prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 
 About me: I'm a 29-year-old man.
 
 My platelet count is low--I've been reading about blood cancers and I'm scared this could be leukemia.
 
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I had routine bloodwork and feel fine. I had a bad cold about 2 weeks ago that has resolved. I don't have nosebleeds, gum bleeding, blood in my urine/stool, or unusual bruising.
 - History: No blood thinners or new medications.
 - Vitals: BP 112/70, HR 74
 - Exam: No abnormal exam findings reported.
 
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Platelets: 128 x10^3/uL (150-400) LOW
 - Hemoglobin: 13.1 g/dL (12.0-16.0) normal
 - WBC: 6.8 x10^3/uL (4.5-11.0) normal
 - INR: 1.0 (0.9-1.1) normal
  I don't have health insurance and can't afford an ER visit.
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your platelet count at 128 is only mildly below the normal range of 150. This is a very common finding and, in the vast majority of cases, is NOT leukemia. Several things are reassuring here: your white blood cell count and hemoglobin are completely normal (in leukemia, these are usually abnormal too), you feel well, your physical exam is normal, and you have no bleeding symptoms. A very common cause of a mildly low platelet count is a recent viral infection — your cold two weeks ago is a likely explanation. Viral illnesses can temporarily suppress platelet production or increase their destruction, and counts typically recover on their own within a few weeks. The best next step is to have your platelets rechecked in 2-4 weeks to confirm they're trending back to normal. There is no emergency here, and this does not require an ER visit.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 92%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): I understand your worry about leukemia, but your results are actually reassuring against that. Your platelet count at 128 is only mildly below the normal range. Importantly, your white blood cell count and hemoglobin are completely normal — in leukemia, you would typically see abnormalities across multiple blood cell lines, not just a mildly low platelet count. A very common cause of a mild, temporary drop in platelets is a recent viral infection, which fits perfectly with your cold two weeks ago. This is called post-viral thrombocytopenia and usually resolves on its own within weeks. You have no bleeding symptoms, no bruising, normal vitals, and a normal exam — all very reassuring. The most reasonable next step is to have your platelets rechecked in a few weeks to confirm they're trending back to normal. There is no emergency here based on what you've shared.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 92%

This is a very common finding and, in the vast majority of cases, is NOT leukemia or another blood cancer. In leukemia, you would typically see other abnormalities too — your white blood cell count and hemoglobin are both completely normal, your exam is normal, and you have no bleeding symptoms. A very common cause of a mildly low platelet count is a recent viral illness, which fits perfectly with your cold two weeks ago. Post-viral platelet suppression is temporary and usually resolves on its own within a few weeks. Your other clotting test (INR) is also normal, which is reassuring. This is not an emergency. EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your platelet count is mildly low at 128, just slightly below the normal cutoff of 150. This is a very common finding and, in a young healthy person who just recovered from a viral illness (your cold), it is most often caused by the virus temporarily suppressing platelet production or increasing platelet turnover. This is called post-viral thrombocytopenia and typically resolves on its own within a few weeks. Importantly, your other blood counts — white blood cells and hemoglobin — are completely normal, which is very reassuring. Leukemia typically causes more dramatic abnormalities across multiple cell lines (low hemoglobin, abnormal WBC, severely low platelets), along with symptoms. You have none of those. You also have no bleeding symptoms, normal vitals, and a normal exam. The most reasonable next step is to have your platelets rechecked in a few weeks to confirm they're trending back to normal.
